title: Intelligence Briefing Dashboard
subtitle: Weekly Conflict Monitor · All Regions
sources: Reuters · AP News · Al Jazeera · ACLED · US Embassy Alerts · LiveUAMap · BNO News · ISW · British Defence Intelligence · Kyiv Post · Sudan Tribune · Irrawaddy · CNN · NBC · CBS · Fox News · HRW · UN News · CFR · Times of Israel · NPR · CNBC · Atlantic Council · Sudan Tribune · US State Dept · OHCHR · Wikipedia · Washington Post · Amnesty International · AEI · Moscow Times · Kyiv Independent

regions.name: Middle East
overview: The US-Iran war has entered a sustained, high-intensity phase with no de-escalation in sight — nine consecutive nights of US strikes as of 19 July, Iranian retaliation now reaching Syria, Jordan and Kuwait simultaneously (20 Jul), and cumulative regional war costs estimated at $37.5 billion.

The Yemen-Saudi front has crossed from proxy skirmishing into direct state-on-state confrontation — the Houthis' 13 July strike on Abha airport, the 20 July formal maritime blockade declaration, and 22 July tanker strikes mark a qualitative escalation now threatening Red Sea shipping and Saudi oil exports.

Lebanon is the one bright spot, showing a genuine if fragile de-escalation mechanism — the 'pilot zone' Israeli withdrawal became operational on 20 July with Lebanese Army deployment into three villages, a rare verified, incrementally-implemented framework rather than a rhetorical ceasefire.

Frontline and transit states are absorbing rising direct-strike risk as the war's geographic footprint widens — Jordan intercepted missiles almost daily this week including a claimed direct strike attempt on US assets at Aqaba, while Iraqi Kurdistan saw its first Erbil drone intercepts since April.

countries.name: Gaza
incidents: 17 Jul — Nuseirat refugee camp: An Israeli strike killed a Palestinian; a follow-up strike on mourners at his funeral hours later killed 8 more and injured 20.
15-16 Jul — Jabaliya refugee camp: An Israeli airstrike on a police station killed a woman and six police officers, part of roughly a dozen killed over the two days.

Ongoing through mid-July — Gaza-wide: Over 1,092 Palestinians have been killed and 3,500+ wounded by Israeli strikes since the nominal October 2025 ceasefire, with last month's strike tempo the highest since the truce began.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained. The funeral double-tap strike on 17 July shows the nominal ceasefire continuing to erode into a pattern of mass-casualty incidents rather than isolated violations. Absent a genuine disarmament/governance breakthrough (Cairo talks remain stalled), expect continued high-tempo strikes calibrated below full re-invasion threshold but sustaining a high monthly Palestinian civilian death toll.

countries.name: Lebanon
incidents: 2 Jul — Nabatieh al-Fawqa/Braashit: Israeli drone and airstrikes continued despite a newly signed withdrawal framework.
6 Jul — Nabatieh al-Fawqa: An Israeli drone strike on a civilian vehicle killed 4, including a school principal, her mother, a domestic worker and a Syrian national.
20 Jul — Southern Lebanon: Israel began a verified, phased withdrawal from 'pilot zone' outposts; the Lebanese Army deployed into Zawtar al-Gharbiyeh, Froun and Srifa as IDF forces vacated.
countries.coa: Downgraded to HIGH from CRITICAL. A verified, monitored phased-withdrawal mechanism is now operationally underway — not just announced — with the Lebanese Army assuming control of vacated villages under the Trilateral Framework. Expect the pilot-zone model to expand incrementally if the initial three villages hold, punctuated by continued Israeli 'targeted' strikes on alleged Hezbollah figures; a collapse trigger would be a significant Hezbollah attack prompting IDF reoccupation.

countries.name: Iran
incidents: 17-19 Jul — Nationwide: The US conducted its 7th through 9th consecutive nights of strikes, with over 100 US air attacks since 8 Jul; Iran's Health Ministry reports 50+ killed and 500+ wounded in this phase.
20 Jul — Eastern Syria/Gulf-wide: Iran struck Syrian territory for the first time in the war and fired ballistic missiles at US assets at Aqaba airport (Jordan) and bases in Kuwait.
13 Jul — Strait of Hormuz: Iran claimed strikes on two commercial tankers amid the full US naval blockade; an IRGC official warned of 'full-scale offensive operations' if strikes continue.
22 Jul — Nationwide: US strikes continued; Defense Secretary Hegseth put the war's cost to the US at $37.5 billion to date.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained, escalation confirmed. Nine consecutive nights of strikes, a war now costed at $37.5bn, and Iranian retaliation reaching Syria, Jordan and Kuwait simultaneously show no near-term off-ramp. Expect continued nightly US strike cycles and Iranian retaliatory salvos against regional bases, Hormuz shipping and Israel/Jordan overflight corridors, with growing regime-stability stress from cumulative casualties.

countries.name: Yemen
incidents: 13 Jul — Sanaa International Airport/Abha, Saudi Arabia: Strikes hit Sanaa airport as an Iranian aircraft carrying a Houthi delegation approached; Houthis retaliated with missile/drone strikes on Saudi Arabia's Abha airport, the first direct attack on Saudi territory since the 2022 truce.
20 Jul — Red Sea/Saudi Arabia: The Houthis formally declared a maritime blockade of Saudi Arabia.
22 Jul — Red Sea: Houthis claimed attacks on two Saudi oil tankers, Encelia and Layla, for allegedly violating the blockade.

20 Jul — Hodeidah governorate: Renewed clashes between Yemeni government-aligned forces and Houthi forces near Jabal Dhubas followed a 5 Jul battle that killed 16 government troops and reportedly 50+ Houthi fighters.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained, confirmed and escalating. The blockade declaration, tanker attacks and renewed ground clashes mark a qualitative shift from four years of deadlock. Expect the Saudi-Houthi truce to remain effectively collapsed, a widening maritime blockade risking Red Sea shipping generally, further ground offensives around Hodeidah threatening the west coast supply corridor, and deepening entanglement with the wider Iran war.

countries.name: Israel
incidents: 19 Jul — Deir Jarir, West Bank: Israeli forces killed two Palestinians during an army-backed settler attack on the town.
7-13 Jul — West Bank-wide: Roughly 65 Palestinians were injured by Israeli forces or settlers; OCHA recorded an average of 6 settler attacks per day in 2026, the highest rate on record.

Ongoing through 19 Jul — Nationwide: Israel remained a direct combatant in the Iran war for a ninth consecutive night, with Jordan and Israel jointly intercepting an Iranian missile transiting toward Israel near Aqaba on 19 Jul.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained. Israel remains engaged on three fronts simultaneously — direct combatant status in the Iran war, near-daily Gaza strikes despite the nominal ceasefire, and record-pace West Bank settler violence under a permissive policy environment. No near-term resolution is visible on any track; watch for a mass-casualty missile strike on an Israeli population center as a potential inflection point.

countries.name: Iraq
incidents: 15 Jul — Erbil: Six drones targeted Erbil International Airport and the US consulate, all intercepted; a separate strike hit al-Harir airbase, triggering Patriot system activation for the first time since April.
11 Jul — Iraq-wide: Kataib Hezbollah publicly restated its refusal to disarm or halt military expansion ahead of the 30 Sep deadline for the US-led anti-ISIS Coalition mission.
Cumulative through 17 Jul — Iraqi Kurdistan: At least 904 drones and missiles have struck the region since the war began, causing 29 deaths and 138 injuries.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained. Attacks have resumed after an April lull but remain below the February-March peak. Expect continued intermittent drone/rocket attacks on Erbil-area US positions and periodic militia rhetoric resisting disarmament ahead of the 30 Sep Coalition deadline — a likely flashpoint that could trigger direct US retaliation inside Iraq.

countries.name: Saudi Arabia
incidents: 13 Jul — Abha International Airport: A Houthi missile and drone strike forced a temporary closure, the first direct Houthi attack on Saudi territory since the 2022 truce.
20 Jul — Nationwide/Red Sea: The Houthis declared a formal maritime blockade against Saudi Arabia, threatening Red Sea oil export flows.
22 Jul — Red Sea: Two Saudi oil tankers, Encelia and Layla, were struck by Houthi forces for allegedly violating the blockade.
countries.coa: Upgraded to CRITICAL from HIGH. The shift from threats to an executed blockade plus confirmed tanker strikes is a material escalation directly threatening Saudi oil exports and territorial airspace, not just militia proxy skirmishing. Expect further attacks on Saudi-flagged or Saudi-bound tankers and possible renewed strikes on airports or oil infrastructure, with Riyadh facing pressure to respond militarily or negotiate amid rising regional oil-price volatility.

countries.name: Syria
incidents: 7 Jul — Damascus: Twin IED blasts near the Four Seasons Hotel during French President Macron's state visit killed 1 and wounded 36.
9 Jul — Damascus: Authorities announced the arrest of the full ISIL-linked cell behind the Damascus bombings, including the earlier lawyers'-cafe bombing that killed 10.
20 Jul — Eastern Syria: Iran struck Syrian territory for the first time in the 2026 war, part of a coordinated regional missile salvo also hitting US assets in Jordan and Kuwait.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained; a new dimension added as Syria is now also a target of direct Iranian strikes, not just ISIL terrorism. Expect continued low-level ISIL-linked cell activity even as the immediate cell is neutralized, plus a new risk vector from Iran using Syrian territory as a strike venue tied to the wider war, raising the possibility of Israeli or US retaliatory strikes on Syrian soil.

countries.name: Egypt
incidents: 15 Jul — Suez Canal-wide: The Suez Canal Authority raised transit surcharges further (crude/petroleum tankers up to 37%, bulk carriers to 22%), reflecting sustained reduced and rerouted traffic.
Ongoing — Sinai: No new kinetic security incidents were identified this window; the peninsula remains free of major attacks.
countries.coa: MODERATE maintained. Egypt's main exposure remains economic rather than security-driven, with continued Suez Canal revenue pressure from rerouted shipping tracking the Yemen/Red Sea situation; a Bab al-Mandeb closure or wider blockade would hit canal revenue hard.

countries.name: Jordan
incidents: 18 Jul — Nationwide: Jordanian air defenses intercepted 10 Iranian missiles aimed at the kingdom overnight; no casualties or damage.
19-20 Jul — Aqaba/Nationwide: Israeli and Jordanian forces jointly intercepted an Iranian missile transiting toward Aqaba on 19 Jul; Jordan's Army intercepted 3 further Iranian missiles on 20 Jul.
22 Jul — Aqaba: Missile alert sirens sounded; the military intercepted 4 missiles while two fell in uninhabited areas, part of an IRGC-claimed strike attempt on US aircraft at Aqaba airport.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained. Interception tempo remains elevated and now includes a direct claimed strike attempt on Aqaba airport, a potential escalation beyond pure transit risk. Expect continued near-daily Iranian missile transits through Jordanian airspace with intermittent direct targeting of US-linked assets; a miss or direct hit could rapidly escalate Jordan from transit corridor to belligerent.

countries.name: Libya
incidents: 13 Jul — Sirte: Rival military chiefs Khaled Haftar (LNA) and Salah Al Din Al Namroush (GNU-aligned) held landmark unification talks under the US-driven 'Boulos initiative.'
Ongoing — Tripoli: No new militia clashes were confirmed this window, though Misurata-based armed groups continue rejecting the US unity plan.
countries.coa: MODERATE maintained. The political process is cautiously advancing under US mediation, but real risk of spoiler action from Misurata-based factions opposed to elevating Haftar remains; watch for a flare-up around discussion of a February 2027 election-timeline target.

countries.name: Tunisia
incidents: 4 Jul — Tunis: Ennahda Secretary-General Ajmi Ourimi and member Mosaab Gharbi were each sentenced to 3 years on terrorism-related charges.

8 Jul — Tunis: 21 people, including Ennahda leaders and former officials, were sentenced to 12-35 years in the 'Conspiracy 2' case; Human Rights Watch published a report condemning the pattern as politically motivated.
countries.coa: MODERATE maintained. This is a political-repression trend rather than a kinetic security incident; expect continued judicial pressure on Ennahda and broader opposition figures with further sentencing waves likely, without near-term policy reversal.

regions.name: Ukraine/Russia
overview: Ukraine's war effort is now entangled with an internal political crisis — Zelensky's back-to-back dismissals of Defense Minister Fedorov and then Commander-in-Chief Syrskyi (21 Jul) triggered the largest domestic protest movement since 2022, running concurrently with the deadliest week yet of Russian Black Sea strikes and a record Russian missile barrage on Kyiv.

Long-range strike campaigns on both sides are intensifying and diversifying targets — Russia's 19 July barrage on Kyiv was its largest of the war, Ukraine is hitting logistics infrastructure deep inside Russia, and a Russian strike on a Turkish-flagged grain ship killed 10, raising the risk of an incident drawing in third-country reactions.

Moldova's political risk has eased with Tofan's government confirmed ahead of its constitutional deadline, while Georgia's authoritarian trajectory deepens with a 600-day protest movement, new political-prisoner designations and a worsening press-freedom crackdown.

The South Caucasus shows a split track — Armenia continues dismantling pro-Russian oligarchic influence domestically while advancing peace-process steps with Baku, whereas Azerbaijan uses its Council of Europe withdrawal threat and expanding European gas exports as leverage.

countries.name: Ukraine
incidents: 16 Jul — Kyiv: Parliament confirmed energy executive Serhiy Koretskyi as PM (289-1) amid protests over the dismissal of Defense Minister Fedorov; hundreds rallied in Kyiv, Lviv, Odesa and Dnipro.
19 Jul — Kyiv: Russia launched its largest ballistic missile barrage on Kyiv of the war — roughly 40 missiles and 125 drones in 53 minutes — killing 1 and injuring 17 across five districts.

19-20 Jul — Black Sea/Odesa: A Russian missile strike on a Turkish-flagged grain carrier leaving Odesa killed 10, the deadliest single incident yet in the weeks-long campaign against merchant shipping.
21 Jul — Kyiv: Zelensky dismissed Commander-in-Chief Syrskyi, replacing him with Mykhailo Drapatyi, as protests continued; organizers set a 24 Jul deadline for Fedorov's reinstatement.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained, compounding. Sustained Russian missile/drone escalation against Kyiv and Black Sea shipping coincides with an unresolved civil-military leadership crisis. If the Fedorov standoff persists past 24 July, expect continued daily protests and possible further cabinet reshuffling, alongside continued Ukrainian deep strikes on Russian refineries and logistics to keep pressure on Russia's fuel crisis.

countries.name: Russia
incidents: 17-18 Jul — Moscow Oblast: A large-scale Ukrainian drone raid started fires at an oil depot in Noginsk and a logistics center in Elektrostal; all four Moscow airports suspended flights.
20 Jul — Moscow: Authorities reported 400+ drones targeting the city in one of the largest single raids of the war.
20 Jul — Moscow Oblast/Kherson: Ukraine struck an oil depot and logistics facilities plus 6-7 'shadow fleet' vessels and an FSB base in occupied Kherson Oblast.
22 Jul — Krasnodar/Stavropol: Ukrainian strikes hit two warehouses Kyiv says supply Russian military drone components, killing at least 1.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained; the fuel crisis has become genuinely nationwide with real civil-unrest potential given rationing and price shocks. Expect continued nationwide fuel rationing as strikes increasingly hit the Moscow region directly rather than only border oblasts — a politically sensitive shift — alongside reciprocal Russian missile/drone escalation against Ukrainian cities.

countries.name: Belarus
incidents: 7-31 Jul — Maloritsky/Drogichinsky districts: Belarus is conducting scheduled mobilization/training exercises with territorial-force conscripts along the Ukraine border.
14-19 Jul — Brest region: Belarus and China held a joint exercise, 'Falcon Assault,' near the Polish (NATO) border.
Ongoing — Nationwide: Lukashenko reiterated Belarusian military facilities are 'vulnerable' to Ukrainian drones but denied any intent to join the war.
countries.coa: MODERATE maintained. Belarus likely continues its balancing act, hosting China-linked drills near the Suwałki Gap while avoiding direct participation in strikes on Ukraine; watch for recurrence or expansion of China-Belarus exercises, which could raise NATO threat perceptions independent of the Ukraine war itself.

countries.name: Moldova
incidents: 21-22 Jul — Chișinău: Parliament approved PM-designate Vasile Tofan's government after a 9-hour session; Tofan was sworn in 22 Jul ahead of the 26 Jul constitutional deadline.
13 Jul — Copanca, Căușeni district: A Russian Shahed/Geran-2 drone crossed into Moldovan airspace and exploded near the village, one of 40+ such violations since the war began.
countries.coa: Downgraded to MODERATE from HIGH. With Tofan's government confirmed and sworn in, the immediate political-uncertainty risk has resolved. Expect a push on EU-accession reforms over the next quarter, though persistent Russian hybrid pressure — drone airspace violations, disinformation — targeting the new pro-EU government remains a background risk.

countries.name: Georgia
incidents: 18 Jul — Tbilisi: The 598th consecutive day of pro-EU protests included a march to parliament in solidarity with Ukraine.
21 Jul — Tbilisi: Transparency International Georgia classified 90 individuals as political prisoners under Council of Europe criteria, including journalist Mzia Amaghlobeli.
22 Jul — Tbilisi: Opposition TV host Vakho Sanaia was sentenced to 14 days over a Facebook post; Georgia has fallen to 135th of 180 in the World Press Freedom Index.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained. Expect continued weekly mass protests alongside an intensifying judicial crackdown on opposition figures and media; a new Lelo/UNM/Girchi opposition coalition could become a mobilization focal point, with the government potentially attempting to ban it via the constitutional court.

countries.name: Armenia
incidents: 16 Jul — Yerevan: The Security Council welcomed deployment of a new EU civilian Partnership Mission to counter disinformation, cyberattacks and illicit finance.
21 Jul — Yerevan: Ararat Cement, owned by jailed pro-Russian oligarch Gagik Tsarukyan, was placed under state management.
23 Jul — Yerevan: Narek Karapetyan, nephew of pro-Russian billionaire Samvel Karapetyan, was charged with coercion and money laundering after losing parliamentary immunity.
countries.coa: MODERATE maintained. Expect Yerevan to continue dismantling pro-Russian oligarchic networks domestically while advancing peace-treaty steps with Baku, pending Armenian constitutional reform expected late 2026/2027; Russia may respond with increased hybrid economic or disinformation pressure.

countries.name: Azerbaijan
incidents: 13 Jul — Shusha: Aliyev said Baku is 'unequivocally considering' full withdrawal from the Council of Europe after PACE suspended its voting rights.
21-22 Jul — Berlin: Aliyev signed a 'Joint Declaration on a Strategic Agenda' with Chancellor Merz and confirmed a 10-year, 1.5 bcm/year gas deal via the Southern Gas Corridor.
countries.coa: LOW maintained. Expect Azerbaijan to continue leveraging gas exports for diplomatic capital in Europe while domestic repression of opposition and press continues; the Council of Europe withdrawal threat likely persists as a pressure tactic without follow-through within 90 days.

regions.name: Sahel
overview: Cross-border insurgent convergence is accelerating — Mali's FLA-JNIM tactical alliance inflicted its heaviest single-incident losses of the month on 18 July at Tabankort, while Niger's Téra attack (17 Jul) and Burkina Faso's Djibo assault (19-20 Jul) confirm JNIM/ISSP retain the initiative across the entire Liptako-Gourma tri-border zone simultaneously.

Sudan's atrocity trajectory is the region's gravest humanitarian risk — UN investigators' genocide-marker findings on El Fasher, plus explicit warnings the same pattern is materializing in El Obeid, and RSF/SAF reinforcement near the Chad border point toward an elevated risk of a new mass-casualty siege within 90 days.

State capacity and international oversight are simultaneously eroding — Burkina Faso's rupture with France and the wind-down of the UN Human Rights Office mean regional monitoring is becoming structurally harder even as violence intensifies, likely understating the true scale of civilian harm.

Humanitarian and migration pressures are compounding conflict risk — Mauritania's deadliest 2026 Atlantic-route shipwreck (144 dead/missing) and Nigeria's worsening Northwest banditry show mass-casualty humanitarian events becoming a near-weekly occurrence across the Sahel belt.

countries.name: Mali
incidents: 18 Jul — Tabankort, Gao region: A JNIM-Azawad Liberation Front coalition ambushed a FAMa/Africa Corps convoy withdrawing from Anéfis toward Gao, killing at least 50 soldiers and capturing dozens more.
22 Jul — Northern Mali: Analysts confirmed the FLA-JNIM tactical alliance is deepening nationwide, with Russia's Africa Corps assessed as not having changed the conflict's overall trajectory.
Ongoing through 17 Jul — Kidal, Tessalit, Tessit and Ber remain outside government control, held by the JNIM-FLA coalition.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained. Expect continued FLA-JNIM joint operations pressing the Kidal-Gao-Timbuktu axis, with a high likelihood of further ambushes on FAMa/Africa Corps resupply and relief columns as government forces attempt to hold recaptured towns; watch for spillover of the FLA-JNIM cooperation model into Niger's Tillabéri corridor.

countries.name: Niger
incidents: 17 Jul — Téra, Tillabéri region: Militants attacked a Nigerien military detachment near the tri-border area with Mali and Burkina Faso, killing 16 soldiers and wounding 23; authorities claim 15 attackers killed.
Ongoing — Tillabéri: The region remains the deadliest civilian conflict zone in the central Sahel following a string of mass-casualty JNIM/ISSP attacks earlier this year.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained. The Téra losses raise the risk of further morale strain within Nigerien forces already showing signs of friction from earlier deployment-refusal reports; given Mali's FLA-JNIM alliance dynamics, there is a moderate risk of cross-border coordination or copy-cat tactics affecting western Niger.

countries.name: Burkina Faso
incidents: 19-20 Jul — Djibo, Sahel region: A large-scale JNIM assault on Burkinabè defense and security force positions was repelled, per state media; independent casualty confirmation was unavailable.

Early Jul (ongoing wave) — Di, Dalan, Tiéré, Solhan, Seguenega: JNIM raids killed at least 22-41 soldiers and militia across several coordinated attacks, part of the same offensive continuing into this window.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained. Expect JNIM to sustain rolling, dispersed attacks on isolated FDS/VDP positions across the Sahel and Boucle du Mouhoun regions rather than a single decisive battle, consistent with its siege-and-attrition strategy against a junta increasingly isolated diplomatically post-France rupture and UN Human Rights Office wind-down.

countries.name: Sudan
incidents: 8 Jul — El Fasher/El Obeid: The UN Fact-Finding Mission reported RSF mass killings, abductions and gang rape in El Fasher bear 'distinct markers of genocide,' and warned the same siege pattern is emerging around El Obeid.

~20 Jul — El Obeid, North Kordofan: RSF drone strikes continued terrorizing the besieged city of roughly 500,000, which has been without electricity for nearly a month; the UAE announced a $30 million emergency humanitarian response.
21 Jul — Tine, North Darfur: A drone strike hit a water source, killing at least 21 civilians, part of an escalating campaign on markets and civilian vehicles in the last army/Joint Force stronghold.
22 Jul — Kalbus/Darfur-Chad border: SAF, the Joint Force and RSF were reported reinforcing positions near the Chad border, raising fears of renewed large-scale fighting.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained. El Obeid is at high and rising risk of replicating the El Fasher atrocity pattern per the UN's own comparative warning. Expect continued drone-strike attrition against Tine and other Darfur/Kordofan holdout towns, with a meaningful probability of a major ground assault on El Obeid or Tine within the quarter absent a ceasefire mechanism.

countries.name: Chad
incidents: 21 Jul — Tine, North Darfur: The drone strike killing 21+ civilians near the Chad border added pressure to an already saturated refugee corridor into Chad's Wadi Fira province.
22 Jul — Chad-Sudan border: SAF, Joint Force and RSF reinforcements near Kalbus/al-Tina raised the risk of renewed fighting that could trigger a fresh refugee surge into eastern Chad.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained. Chad's exposure will track developments in North/West Darfur; the reinforcement buildup near Kalbus/al-Tina suggests a material chance of renewed ground clashes within 90 days, likely pushing a new wave of refugees into Wadi Fira and Ennedi-Est, further straining already-underfunded camps.

countries.name: Mauritania
incidents: 18-21 Jul — Off Nouadhibou: UNHCR reported 144 migrants and refugees dead or missing after a boat that departed Gambia drifted for 25 days before reaching Nouadhibou with 38 survivors — the deadliest incident on the West African Atlantic migration route in 2026.
14-18 Jul — Off Nouadhibou: Three separate rescue operations saved 387 people from other distressed vessels in the same period.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained. Mauritania's role as the primary departure point on the world's deadliest migration route will keep generating recurring mass-casualty events at sea, with associated strain on coast guard and rescue capacity and potential diplomatic friction with the EU/Spain over Atlantic-route enforcement.

countries.name: Nigeria
incidents: 18-19 Jul — Borno State: Troops repelled an ISWAP attack on a military base and thwarted a mass-abduction attempt at Federal Government Girls College, Monguno, rescuing 46 students.

18 Jul — Maiduguri, Borno: State government confirmed receipt of a proof-of-life video for students still held from the 30 Jun Lassa school abduction; at least 36 students and one staff member remain captive.
20-21 Jul — Sokoto/Zamfara: Operation FANSAN YAMMA rescued 27 kidnap victims and foiled multiple abduction attempts.
22-23 Jul — Zamfara/Sokoto: Bandits killed at least 20 in a coordinated assault in Zamfara and abducted 20+ IDPs, killing 3, from Gangara village in Sokoto.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained. Borno remains a dual-track threat environment — ISWAP infiltration/abduction attempts near Maiduguri continuing alongside slow progress on the remaining Lassa captives. The Northwest shows an escalating trend of large-casualty banditry despite active counter-banditry operations, indicating troop rescues are not yet suppressing overall attack tempo.

regions.name: Asia-Pacific
overview: Gray-zone escalation is broadening beyond the South China Sea into the Taiwan Strait and Second Thomas Shoal — the first-ever PLA helicopter median-line crossing (21 Jul) and the first direct physical assault on Philippine military personnel at Second Thomas Shoal (20 Jul) both mark qualitative escalations beyond prior force-on-force thresholds.

Trilateral US-Japan-ROK coordination against North Korea is intensifying diplomatically even as Pyongyang continues DMZ fortification and possible missile-infrastructure construction, signaling a stable but unresolved standoff rather than de-escalation.

Japan's nuclear posture debate has moved from fringe to mainstream policy discourse — a ruling coalition partner's push for nuclear-powered submarines and the Defense Minister's public call for a nuclear weapons debate reflect a significant potential shift tied to revision of Japan's three core security documents.

Internal conflict intensity remains highest in Myanmar and Pakistan, both sustaining CRITICAL-level violence, while South and Southeast Asian flashpoints — Manipur, Papua, Bangladesh, Sri Lanka — continue at a simmering, sub-critical but unresolved level.

countries.name: North Korea
incidents: 16 Jul — Washington: The 23rd US-ROK-Japan Trilateral Chiefs of Defense meeting reaffirmed trilateral security cooperation and the goal of North Korea's complete denuclearization.
17 Jul — DMZ/Kaesong area: Satellite analysis identified at least 21 identical drive-through structures near the DMZ, 52km from Seoul, suspected truck-based missile/rocket launch infrastructure.
22 Jul — Manila: US, South Korean and Japanese foreign ministers met at ASEAN meetings, reiterating the denuclearization commitment.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained. No new missile test was verified within the window itself, but trilateral diplomatic/military activity and continued DMZ militarization sustain elevated tension; expect further weapons-system tests tied to Pyongyang's five-year plan, especially around the new destroyer's induction.

countries.name: Myanmar
incidents: 11 Jul — Lin Ta Lu village, Sagaing Region: A junta airstrike on a monastery sheltering over 150 displaced people killed at least 22.
18-19 Jul — Rakhine, Mandalay and Sagaing Regions: A wave of junta airstrikes and drone attacks killed 6 civilians, including children and monks, and injured 26+.
Through 19 Jul — Nationwide: The verified toll reached 274 civilian deaths from airstrikes since 20 April 2026, with 579 injured.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained. No de-escalation indicators. Expect continued near-daily junta airstrikes targeting resistance-held and flood-affected areas through the election period, rising civilian casualty counts, and continued ASEAN statements without enforcement mechanisms.

countries.name: Pakistan
incidents: 16 Jul — Balochistan: Operation Shaban's toll reached 129 militants killed since 5 July.
21 Jul — Azad Kashmir: JAAC suspended its planned 'Long March' to Muzaffarabad after backchannel talks yielded assurances on refugee assembly-seat grievances.
22 Jul — Balochistan (Surab/Mastung): 10 more militants were killed, including 2 female suicide bombers apprehended before detonation, bringing Operation Shaban's toll to 139.
Ongoing — Azad Kashmir: A three-phase general election begins 27 July under heavy security and communications restrictions, following months of protest violence that killed 30+.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained. Operation Shaban is likely to continue grinding through August with the toll climbing past 150; the 27 July AJK election is likely to proceed under heavy restrictions, with post-election unrest plausible if JAAC's core demands remain unresolved.

countries.name: India
incidents: 20 Jul — Imphal East district: Gunmen killed a man and critically wounded his wife in a targeted attack at their residence.
20 Jul — Ukhrul district: Security forces destroyed two militant bunkers and recovered arms and ammunition in a joint operation.
20 Jul — Chandel district: Three cadres of the banned KYKL outfit were arrested near the India-Myanmar border.
countries.coa: MODERATE maintained. Violence remains localized and sporadic — targeted killings, small-scale militant activity — rather than large-scale ethnic clashes within the window; expect continued elevated security force presence in Kamjong, Ukhrul and Chandel districts without a durable political resolution.

countries.name: China
incidents: 4 Jul — East of Taiwan: China rotated its coast guard patrol task group, confirming a standing patrol 137-230km off Taiwan's east coast within Taiwan's claimed EEZ.
7 Jul — Senkaku Islands: Chinese and Japanese coast guard vessels faced off; each side accused the other of intrusion.
6-13 Jul — Qingdao/Yellow Sea: The China-Russia 'Joint Sea-2026' naval exercise concluded, with participating forces proceeding to a joint Pacific patrol.
21 Jul — Taiwan Strait: A PLA helicopter crossed the median line for what analysts call a first-ever gray-zone incident, flying inside Taiwan's restricted zone alongside a drone for over 9 hours.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained, trending toward the top of the band. Beijing is normalizing higher-order signaling — routine coast guard patrols near Taiwan, joint exercises with Russia, and now rotary-wing median-line crossings — as a steady-state 'new normal'; Senkaku standoffs are likely to keep recurring with rising risk of miscalculation.

countries.name: Taiwan
incidents: 1 Jul — Keelung: The Nvidia chip-smuggling investigation expanded to 9 suspects; prosecutors raided 12 sites including Super Micro Taiwan over alleged smuggling of ~50 AI servers to China.
4 Jul — Taiwan Strait: Taiwan deployed coast guard vessels to monitor China's newly rotated standing patrol off its east coast.
21 Jul — Taiwan Strait: A PLA helicopter and drone operated on Taiwan's side of the median line for over 9 hours, the first confirmed helicopter median-line crossing.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained. Expect the PLA to continue testing new gray-zone precedents in the run-up to and during Han Kuang Exercise No. 42 (5 Aug), likely prompting a Taiwanese show-of-readiness response; the chip-smuggling investigation will likely yield further indictments and continued US-Taiwan friction over export-control enforcement.

countries.name: Philippines
incidents: 20 Jul — Second Thomas Shoal: A Philippine sailor was injured in a melee after a China Coast Guard cutter approached the BRP Sierra Madre — the first documented direct physical violence against Philippine military personnel in the dispute.
23 Jul — Scarborough Shoal: China said it blocked Philippine vessels from approaching the shoal.
7 Jul — Manila: The Philippines confirmed a deal with Japan to acquire five retired Abukuma-class destroyer escorts, cost-free, under Japan's Official Security Assistance framework.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained, elevated escalation risk flagged. The 20 July Second Thomas Shoal incident is a qualitative escalation beyond prior water-cannon tactics; expect continued high-frequency CCG presence at both Scarborough and Second Thomas Shoal with a meaningful chance of further physical confrontations, while the destroyer transfer strengthens capacity only over a 2-3 year horizon.

countries.name: South Korea
incidents: 13 Jul — Nationwide: Cyberattack attempts against the South Korean military hit a fresh record high, following 2025's five-year high of 18,951 attempts.
16 Jul — Washington: ROK Joint Chiefs Chairman Gen. Jin Yong-sung joined the trilateral defense chiefs meeting reaffirming cooperation against North Korean threats.
Ongoing — Nationwide: The DPRK-linked Kimsuky group continued targeting South Korean software vendors, breaching their downstream customers.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained, driven primarily by North Korea's escalating cyber operations. Expect DPRK cyber operations against South Korean military and defense-industrial targets to keep intensifying, with AI-enabled deepfake and social-engineering tradecraft becoming standard rather than exceptional.

countries.name: Vietnam
incidents: 12 Jul — Hanoi/Manila: Vietnam and the Philippines marked the 50th anniversary of diplomatic relations amid continued implementation of their June 2026 Enhanced Strategic Partnership.
19 Jul — South China Sea: Vietnam accused the Chinese survey vessel Haiyang Dizhi 8 and its escorts of violating its EEZ and continental shelf, demanding withdrawal.
countries.coa: MODERATE maintained. Expect continued Chinese survey and coast guard activity within Vietnam's claimed EEZ, met with formal protests but no kinetic response from Hanoi, consistent with its balancing act; the Philippines partnership will likely deepen further.

countries.name: Japan
incidents: 7 Jul — Senkaku Islands: A coast guard face-off with China saw Japan say it expelled two Chinese vessels approaching a Japanese fishing boat.
9 Jul — Tokyo: Coalition partner Japan Innovation Party formally proposed acquiring nuclear-powered submarines and reviewing Japan's non-nuclear principle.
19 Jul — Tokyo: Defense Minister Shinjirō Koizumi said Japan 'cannot avoid' a national debate on nuclear weapons.
countries.coa: MODERATE maintained, watch for upgrade. Senkaku confrontations are becoming a near-monthly pattern, and Japan's own posture is shifting toward more assertive self-reliant defense planning; the nuclear submarine debate is unlikely to produce a policy change within 90 days but will keep generating domestic political friction.

countries.name: Indonesia
incidents: 17 Jul — Dekai District, Yahukimo Regency, Papua: A security operation against a TPNPB-OPM battalion killed three militants; the manhunt continues for a wanted commander.
Ongoing — Yahukimo: Indonesia has tripled its troop deployment following a rise in security incidents from 3 (early 2025) to 23 (same period 2026).
countries.coa: HIGH maintained, validated by this week's events. Indonesia is likely to intensify TNI operations in Yahukimo as the manhunt continues, raising the risk of further clashes and displacement; aviation remains a persistent vulnerability given the region's dependence on small aircraft.

countries.name: Thailand
incidents: Ongoing — Thailand-Cambodia border: No new dated incident was found this window; the ceasefire brokered after the July 2025 border war continues to hold, most recently reaffirmed in December 2025.
countries.coa: LOW maintained. Absent a new triggering incident, the border situation remains a low-simmer risk rather than active conflict; landmine incidents remain the most probable trigger for renewed escalation.

countries.name: Bangladesh
incidents: 22 Jul — Dhaka: The BNP publicly distanced itself from the July National Charter-backed reform agenda, deepening its rift with the NCP and Jamaat over constitutional reform implementation.
Ongoing — Nationwide: Rights group ASK has documented at least 66 politically-related killings and 61 deaths in police custody over the preceding six months.
countries.coa: MODERATE maintained. Expect continued low-grade political violence and custodial deaths under the BNP government, with the widening BNP-NCP/Jamaat rift over constitutional reform likely to generate further public friction and possible street mobilization.

countries.name: Sri Lanka
incidents: 6 Jul — Colombo: The government formed a three-member investigative committee into the early-July Negombo prison violence.
11-12 Jul — Colombo/Geneva: The UN Human Rights Office and Amnesty International urged a prompt, independent investigation into torture allegations against transferred Negombo inmates.
Ongoing — No new dated incidents were confirmed within this window; the situation remains in an investigative and fallout phase following the early-July riot.
countries.coa: MODERATE maintained. Expect the government-appointed commission to proceed slowly, with continued international pressure for transparency; risk of further unrest is elevated if the investigation stalls or additional custody deaths emerge.

regions.name: Americas
overview: Venezuela's institutional crisis has deepened rather than resolved — Acting President Rodríguez is governing past her own constitutional mandate expiry while the earthquake toll approaches 5,000 and CFR analysts rank Venezuela among the world's top global conflict risks.

Two nearly simultaneous presidential transitions — Colombia 7 Aug, Peru 28 Jul — are occurring under acute stress: Colombia's transition remains formally suspended amid mutual coup accusations, while Peru's Fujimori is already proposing Bukele-style mega-prisons before taking office.

Non-state armed groups are sustaining high operational tempo despite state countermeasures — Haiti's Gang Suppression Force remains at roughly 18% of authorized strength even as the UN warns of further mass killings, while Colombia's ELN and Ecuador's cartels continue high-tempo operations.

Cuba's cyclical grid-collapse/protest pattern has become the region's most sustained civil-unrest trend, now exceeding the scale of the July 2021 protests, contrasting with Jamaica and the Dominican Republic as comparatively stable low-threat outliers.

countries.name: Venezuela
incidents: 4 Jul — Caracas: Acting President Delcy Rodríguez's constitutional 180-day interim mandate lapsed with no government response; opposition and constitutional scholars call continued rule unlawful.
16 Jul — Nationwide: The earthquake death toll from the 24 June twin quakes rose to nearly 5,000, with over 16,700 injured and tens of thousands still displaced.

21 Jul — Regional: CFR's 2026 Conflict Risk Assessment elevated Venezuela to a top-five global flashpoint, flagging that roughly 64 US strikes on Caribbean/Pacific smuggling boats since September raise risk of escalation to Venezuelan territory.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained, pressure compounding. Expect continued institutional drift under Rodríguez absent a negotiated electoral path, with earthquake recovery remaining a chronic stressor on public order. Maritime interdiction operations are likely to continue at pace; a shift to direct territorial strikes remains a tail-risk. Monitor closely around 28 July for symbolic mobilization.

countries.name: Haiti
incidents: 4-9 Jul — Kenscoff/Grand Fond: Gang attacks killed at least 61 people, including 14 children, and displaced roughly 5,840.
17 Jul — International: The UN issued a fresh warning of mass-killing risk in Haiti, citing continued gang territorial expansion.
20 Jul — UN Security Council: The Special Representative told the Council Haiti's transition is 'at a critical moment'; the Gang Suppression Force remains at only ~1,000 of 5,550 authorized troops.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained. Gang control of Port-au-Prince will likely continue outpacing Gang Suppression Force deployment; expect further mass-casualty incidents in West Department and continued high sexual-violence and child-recruitment rates, with elections planning remaining secondary to the security emergency.

countries.name: Colombia
incidents: 7 Jul — Bogotá: President-elect Abelardo de la Espriella suspended the transition process, accusing outgoing President Petro of coup-plotting after Petro refused to recognize the 21 Jun runoff result; no evidence of fraud was substantiated.
14 Jul — Chocó: The ELN abducted 39 civilians from a highway bus; a same-day army rescue freed all hostages, but 2 soldiers were killed.
22 Jul — Bogotá: The US Embassy issued a security alert reflecting continued elevated crime concern ahead of the 7 Aug handover.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained. Expect the Petro-De la Espriella standoff to remain unresolved or only partially patched together ahead of the 7 August handover, raising risk of a contested or irregular transfer of power; the ELN will likely sustain a high operational tempo in Chocó and Arauca as the incoming government's security posture is tested early.

countries.name: Cuba
incidents: 6-14 Jul — Nationwide: Cuba suffered its third total grid collapse in under 10 days, leaving roughly 10 million without power for 24+ hours each time.
3 Jul — Havana: Activist Leonardo Romero Negrín was violently arrested during a cacerolazo protest, drawing public outrage.

Ongoing (June-July) — Nationwide: The Cuban Conflict Observatory recorded 107 street protests in June alone, a record high; protest slogans have escalated from demands for food and electricity to 'Down with the dictatorship.'
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained, now the largest sustained protest wave since July 2021. Expect recurring grid collapses to continue driving cyclical protest waves through the US fuel blockade period, with government response likely remaining repression-heavy rather than substantive relief.

countries.name: Ecuador
incidents: 2 Jul — Manta: A municipal IT analyst was shot dead in broad daylight in the city center.
8 Jul — Manta: Gunmen fired 60+ rounds into a wake, killing 4 — the latest in a string of sicariato attacks, 16 in Manabí in 2026 through early July.
16 Jul — Portoviejo-El Rodeo highway: A 47-year-old man was shot dead while driving.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained. Manabí is likely to remain Ecuador's deadliest province as Los Lobos and Los Choneros continue contesting trafficking corridors; Noboa's 60-day state of emergency across 10 provinces is likely to be extended without materially reducing violence.

countries.name: Peru
incidents: 17 Jul — Los Olivos, Lima: A bus driver was shot and wounded by two gunmen in an extortion-linked attack.
19 Jul — Puente Piedra, Lima: Two interprovincial buses were fired on by motorcycle gunmen; one driver was injured.
22 Jul — Lima: President-elect Keiko Fujimori called for Bukele-style 'mega-prisons' as part of her incoming security platform ahead of her 28 Jul inauguration.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained but watch-listed upward. Expect continued extortion-related shootings of transport workers through and beyond the 28 July inauguration; Fujimori's early security agenda will likely take weeks-to-months to show effect, leaving near-term violence levels largely unchanged.

countries.name: Bolivia
incidents: 6-7 Jul — La Paz: Prosecutors sought 6 months' pretrial detention for farmers'-federation leader Vicente Salazar, transferred to maximum-security Chonchocoro prison on terrorism-related charges tied to the blockade campaign.
22 Jul — La Paz: The Economy Minister said Bolivia is 'days away' from a $2.5-2.8 billion IMF Extended Fund Facility deal following its recent shift off the fixed dollar peg.
countries.coa: MODERATE maintained. Watch for potential renewed blockade or protest activity if Salazar's prosecution is perceived as political persecution, or if IMF-linked austerity terms trigger a repeat of the May-June unrest; a concluded IMF deal by August would ease near-term economic pressure.

countries.name: Dominican Republic
incidents: 22 Jul — Haina/border corridor: Mass deportation operations continued, with overcrowded trucks carrying Haitians to the border under the roughly 10,000/week policy, again criticized by rights observers.
countries.coa: LOW maintained. No organized armed-conflict or mass-unrest indicators; the deportation campaign is likely to continue drawing international criticism without altering the DR's own low domestic-crime classification.

countries.name: Jamaica
incidents: 11 Jul — Nationwide: 291 murders were recorded year-to-date, 83 fewer than the same period in 2025, a 22% decline; shootings down 27%.
Early Jul — Nationwide: The JCF's murder clearance rate reached 111% year-to-date, with the murder rate now 24 per 100,000, closer to the regional average of 15.
countries.coa: LOW maintained and trending favorably. The homicide decline is likely to continue absent a major disruption, aided by improved clearance rates; the chief external risk is Jamaica's own troop commitment to Haiti's Gang Suppression Force.
